role_hints.oews_basis: 17-1012 Landscape Architects does NOT appear in the record's 4-digit 5413 top-10 (which is engineering-dominated: 17-2051 Civil Engineers 12.8%) — the signature occupation sits in the unlisted tail; within 541320 proper, licensed LAs plus principals are plausibly ~40-50% of wages (hypothesis)
role_hints.note: Stamped grading/stormwater/public-realm documents, agency approvals and client relationships stay human; planting plans, renderings and specification production are AI-exposed.

role_hints.oews_basis: 17-3011 Architectural and Civil Drafters 3.28% at 4-digit plus design staff in the tail; hypothesis ~15-20% here
role_hints.note: Site-plan production, planting schedules and renders are directly automatable — generative site-design tools target exactly this.
Agency coordination and approvals persist; documentation and submittal paperwork automates.

role_hints.oews_basis: 47-4011 Construction and Building Inspectors 2.05% at 4-digit plus field staff in tail; hypothesis ~10%
role_hints.note: Physical site presence for observation, plant-material approval and punch lists.

role_hints.oews_basis: not in the 5413 top-10; est. remainder ~15% (hypothesis)
role_hints.note: RFP responses and proposals dominate; highly automatable.

source_hints.source: Deltek Clarity Architecture & Engineering Industry Study (annual)
source_hints.why: AI-adoption chapters covering the A&E professions; check whether landscape architecture firms are in the sample frame.

source_hints.source: ASLA (American Society of Landscape Architects) business and technology surveys
source_hints.why: Trade-body data specific to LA firms; verify current survey cadence — ASLA's business-survey output has been intermittent.

source_hints.source: ASLA practice/business survey data on prime-vs-subconsultant mix and fee structures
source_hints.why: The capture question turns on who holds the prime contract; ASLA is the only body likely to have measured it.

source_hints.source: Zweig Group ownership-transition and salary surveys; CLARB licensure data
source_hints.why: Principal age/transition data (Zweig covers A/E broadly) and licensee counts by state.

source_hints.source: Morrissey Goodale AE M&A data; Zweig Group Valuation Report
source_hints.why: LA firms appear as bolt-ons in A&E deals; small-firm multiples with the usual pre-owner-comp caveat.

source_hints.source: NALP (National Association of Landscape Professionals) design-build market data
source_hints.why: Landscape contractors' design-build offerings are the bypass channel for discretionary design work. Acronym trap: this NALP is the contractors' association, not the law-placement body.

capture_questions: Fees are lump-sum or hourly, and a large share of firms work as sub-consultants to architects/engineers/developers who hold the prime contract: when AI cuts design-production hours, does the prime simply re-scope or re-bid the LA sub-fee downward (pass-through), or do prime-held relationships and stamped-document requirements let the LA firm keep the saving? Split the answer by prime vs. sub revenue.
Is the firm prime to the owner/municipality, or a swap-able sub-consultant — who owns the client relationship, and does it survive a principal's exit?

Does residential and commercial design-build by landscape contractors bypass landscape architects except where stamped grading/stormwater documents are required — and can clients generate discretionary amenity/planting design with AI tools directly?
What share of fees is code-required work (stormwater, ADA, public procurement, entitlement drawings) versus discretionary design — i.e., how big is the regulated core that AI cannot bypass?
terminal_value_question: Discretionary planting and amenity design is highly AI-exposed and, in title-act states, not even legally reserved to licensees — the work may be done unlicensed as long as the title 'landscape architect' is not used. Durable paid demand concentrates in stamped, code-required site work (grading, stormwater, public projects). The deciding mechanism is the practice-act vs. title-act split by state: how much of the current fee pool sits inside a genuine practice-act monopoly, and does the rest survive mature AI as a paid service at all? Distinguish AI cutting the firm's production cost from AI (plus contractor design-build) replacing the discretionary product.
special_notes: Role-mix warning: the provided role_mix is 4-digit 5413 and omits 17-1012 Landscape Architects from the top-10 entirely — it describes the engineering-dominated parent, not this code; rebuild shares and flag as hypothesis.

Licensure nuance is the moat question: nearly all states license LAs, but a material subset have title acts only (the work itself can be done unlicensed) — materially weaker than 541310/541330 practice acts; verify the practice-act vs. title-act state split before scoring capture.
Sub-consultant economics: where the architect/engineer/developer holds the prime, the LA firm is a fee-taker — score C on who owns the prime contract, not on end-client demand.

Very small firms (3,549 of 5,041 under 5 employees) with thin transferable EBITDA; n_band is only 46, and the record's 500+ class shows LOWER revenue/firm ($9.1M) than the 100-499 class ($23.6M) — a suppression/noise artifact in SUSB data; treat size-class tails cautiously.
Demand rides construction and municipal spending cycles — normalize any single-year evidence.

Boundary confusion: landscape contractors (design-build installers) are 561730, architecture 541310, civil engineering 541330 — surveys and comps frequently blend LA firms into 'A&E' or into landscaping services; check the sample frame.
